title: System Baselining
controlText: Establish and maintain baseline configurations and inventories of organizational systems (including hardware, software, firmware, and documentation) throughout the respective system development life cycles.
plainEnglish: Know exactly what is on every system — every piece of hardware, every installed application, every firmware version, every configuration setting — and document it as the approved baseline. Keep that documentation current as systems change. Without a baseline you cannot detect unauthorized changes, enforce hardening, or recover quickly from incidents. This is the foundation of the CM family: every other CM control either builds on this baseline or enforces it.

objectives.text: a baseline configuration is established;
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented baseline configuration for each system class (workstations, servers, network gear, mobile devices, cloud environments) that specifies approved software, configuration parameters, network placement, and version/patch levels. The baseline must be formally approved, not just a snapshot someone saved.
commonEvidence: Approved baseline configuration documents per system type
Security configuration checklists or STIGs used as baselines
Configuration Management Plan documenting baseline establishment
Group Policy or configuration-as-code (Ansible, Chef, Puppet) baseline definitions

objectives.text: the baseline configuration includes hardware, software, firmware, and documentation;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Baseline document explicitly covers all four components — hardware specs, approved software list with versions, firmware versions, and supporting documentation. Partial baselines (e.g., software only, no firmware) are a finding.
commonEvidence: Baseline document showing hardware, software, firmware, and documentation sections
CMDB or asset inventory with all four component types
System security plan referencing the baseline

objectives.text: the baseline configuration is maintained (reviewed and updated) throughout the system development life cycle;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence of periodic baseline reviews and updates — calendar reminders, version history on configuration documents, change management records showing baseline updates triggered by significant changes or scheduled reviews.
commonEvidence: Baseline version history or revision log
Periodic baseline review records
Change management tickets showing baseline update as required step

objectives.text: a system inventory is established;
objectives.whatToLookFor: A maintained inventory of system components — physical and virtual — that can be cross-checked against what is actually deployed. Not just an old spreadsheet; an authoritative record used operationally.
commonEvidence: CMDB or asset inventory export
Endpoint management platform inventory (SCCM, Intune, Jamf)
Network discovery scan results
Cloud resource inventory (AWS Config, Azure Resource Manager export)

objectives.text: the system inventory includes hardware, software, firmware, and documentation; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory covers all four component types for each system. Software inventory must include version numbers and license information. Networked devices must include MAC addresses and network addresses.
commonEvidence: Software inventory with version numbers from endpoint management tool
Hardware inventory including serial numbers, manufacturer, model
Firmware version tracking for network gear and appliances

objectives.text: the inventory is maintained (reviewed and updated) throughout the system development life cycle.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory kept current — automated discovery where possible, periodic manual review otherwise. Evidence that decommissioned assets are removed and new assets are added promptly. Stale inventories are a very common finding.
commonEvidence: Automated discovery tool configuration (Nessus, Qualys, Lansweeper)
Periodic inventory reconciliation records
Onboarding/offboarding procedure referencing inventory update steps

commonArtifacts: Baseline configuration documents per system class
CMDB or asset management platform export
Endpoint management tool inventory (SCCM, Intune, Jamf, JAMF Pro)
Change management records showing baseline maintenance
System Security Plan section on CM baseline
commonGaps: Baseline exists for servers but not for workstations, network gear, or cloud environments
Software inventory tracks installed applications but not firmware versions
Baseline document not reviewed or updated after major system changes
CMDB maintained by IT but security is not notified when it changes
Cloud resource inventory exists but is not linked to the on-prem baseline framework
Inventory is manual and months out of date — nobody owns the update process
Baseline says 'current patch level' rather than specifying actual versions — not verifiable

title: Security Configuration Enforcement
controlText: Establish and enforce security configuration settings for information technology products employed in organizational systems.
plainEnglish: Define the specific security settings for each type of IT product in the environment and enforce them. This goes beyond knowing what software is installed (CM.L2-3.4.1) — it specifies the actual security parameters: registry values, account settings, service states, port configurations, authentication requirements. Deviations from the approved settings must be documented and approved, not quietly tolerated. Industry benchmarks like CIS or DISA STIGs are standard sources for the settings themselves.

objectives.text: security configuration settings for information technology products employed in the system are established and included in the baseline configuration; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Security configuration settings derived from a recognized source (CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs, vendor hardening guides) and incorporated into the baseline from CM.L2-3.4.1. Settings must be specific enough to verify — 'minimize services' is not sufficient; 'Telnet service disabled' is.
commonEvidence: CIS Benchmark or DISA STIG compliance reports
Documented deviations from benchmark with business justification
Security configuration checklist per system type
Group Policy Objects (GPOs) showing security settings

objectives.text: security configuration settings for information technology products employed in the system are enforced.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement — not just documentation. Configuration management tools (GPO, Ansible, SCCM baselines, cloud policy) enforce settings and detect or remediate drift. Evidence of enforcement: compliance reports, drift detection alerts, remediation tickets.
commonEvidence: Endpoint compliance reports showing policy conformance percentage
GPO resultant-set-of-policy output for representative endpoints
Configuration drift detection alerts and remediation records
Cloud security posture management (CSPM) compliance reports

commonArtifacts: CIS Benchmark or DISA STIG compliance reports
Endpoint compliance baseline reports (SCCM, Intune, Qualys PC)
commonGaps: Security configuration settings defined in a document but not technically enforced — relies on manual compliance
Servers hardened; workstations, network gear, and cloud resources not covered
Deviations from the benchmark exist but are not documented or approved
Benchmark applied at build time but configuration drift never detected afterward
STIG or CIS used as reference but implementation only meets a fraction of the controls with no tracking of what is skipped
No formal deviation management process — exceptions accumulate silently

title: System Change Management
controlText: Track, review, approve or disapprove, and log changes to organizational systems.
plainEnglish: Every change to systems — hardware upgrades, software installs, configuration changes, patches, network modifications — goes through a formal process: it gets tracked in a ticketing system, reviewed by relevant stakeholders, explicitly approved or rejected, and logged. Ad-hoc changes that bypass the process are a finding. The change log is also the audit trail that lets you reconstruct what was in place at any given time, which matters enormously during incident investigation.

objectives.text: changes to the system are tracked;
objectives.whatToLookFor: A change tracking system (ITSM/ticketing tool) where all proposed system changes are recorded. Coverage: hardware, software, configuration, and firmware changes. Not just major changes — minor config updates and patches should also be tracked.
commonEvidence: Change management tickets from ITSM tool (ServiceNow, Jira, Freshservice)
RFC (Request for Change) records
Patch management records showing tracked deployments

objectives.text: changes to the system are reviewed;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that proposed changes are reviewed by relevant stakeholders before implementation — Change Advisory Board (CAB) minutes, peer review records, security review sign-offs on high-risk changes.
commonEvidence: CAB meeting minutes or agendas showing change review
Change ticket records showing reviewer sign-off
Security review records for significant changes

objectives.text: changes to the system are approved or disapproved; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Each change has an explicit approval decision recorded — not just 'reviewed' but a clear authorized decision. Rejected changes tracked as well. Emergency change approval path documented.
commonEvidence: Change tickets showing approval status and approver name
Escalation records for emergency changes
Rejected change records

objectives.text: changes to the system are logged.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Audit logs of changes including activities before and after the change, who made the change, and timestamps. System-generated change logs (Windows Event Log, Linux audit log, cloud activity logs) supplement the ITSM records.
commonEvidence: Change management tool completion records
System audit logs showing configuration changes
Automated deployment logs from patch management or IaC tools

commonArtifacts: System audit logs showing configuration changes
commonGaps: Changes tracked in a spreadsheet or email rather than a formal ITSM tool
Emergency changes bypass approval process without documentation
Patch management treated as routine maintenance outside the change process
Changes approved but not logged — no audit trail of what actually changed
Cloud configuration changes not fed into the change management process
CAB meets monthly but emergency changes fill the gaps with no equivalent oversight

title: Security Impact Analysis
controlText: Analyze the security impact of changes prior to implementation.
plainEnglish: Before a change goes in, someone with security expertise evaluates what it might do to the security posture — not just whether it will work technically. The person who submitted the change should not be the only reviewer. Security impact analysis is part of the change management process from CM.L2-3.4.3, but specifically focuses on security implications: does this change introduce vulnerabilities, weaken a compensating control, open a network path, or create an exception to a security policy?

objectives.text: the security impact of changes to the system is analyzed prior to implementation.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that security impact analysis happens before changes are implemented — not just technical testing. The analysis is performed by someone with information security responsibilities, distinct from the change submitter. High-risk changes get documented security review; routine patches may follow a streamlined assessment.
commonEvidence: Change tickets with completed security impact analysis section
Security review sign-off records on changes above a risk threshold
Documented criteria for when a full security impact analysis is required
Test environment validation records showing changes were tested before production

commonArtifacts: Change management policy including security impact analysis requirement
Change ticket template with security impact section
Security review records for significant changes
Risk acceptance records for changes approved with known security trade-offs
commonGaps: Security impact analysis only done for major changes; routine patches and config updates skipped
The change submitter completes the security impact section — no independent security review
Template includes a security impact section but it is always left blank or filled with 'none'
No test environment — changes go directly to production
Security review exists but happens after implementation ('post-hoc approval')

title: Access Restrictions for Change
controlText: Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to organizational systems.
plainEnglish: Not everyone should be able to make changes to systems, even if they can technically access them. This practice requires explicitly defining who is authorized to make physical changes (access to server rooms, network closets, datacenter racks) and logical changes (configuration, software installs, patching), documenting those authorizations, and technically enforcing them. It is closely related to least privilege (AC.L2-3.1.5) but focused specifically on the change-making capability and tied into the CM process.

objectives.text: physical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented definition of who may physically access and modify hardware — server rooms, wiring closets, network racks. Should include third-party technicians and how they are escorted and supervised.
commonEvidence: Physical access control policy for server rooms and network areas
Documented authorized personnel list for physical access
Visitor and contractor escort procedures

objectives.text: physical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are documented;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Written records showing physical access authorizations — not just a policy, but the actual personnel list or badge-access configuration.
commonEvidence: Badge access authorization records
Physical access control system roster
Visitor log for datacenter or server room access

objectives.text: physical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are approved;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Physical access grants are explicitly approved — not automatically inherited from job title. Temporary access for vendors or contractors is time-limited and approved before the visit.
commonEvidence: Physical access approval records
Temporary access request and approval records for vendors

objectives.text: physical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are enforced;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement — badge readers, locks, camera coverage — that actually prevents unauthorized physical access. Not just a 'do not enter' sign.
commonEvidence: Physical access control system configuration
Physical security audit or assessment records

objectives.text: logical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented definition of who may make logical changes — install software, change configurations, apply patches, modify network rules. Distinct from who can read systems.
commonEvidence: Change authorization matrix
RBAC definitions showing change-capable roles
Patch management tool access definitions

objectives.text: logical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are documented;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Written records of who holds change-capable logical access. Maintained and reviewed.
commonEvidence: Group membership exports for admin and change roles
Privileged access inventory
Patch management tool user roster

objectives.text: logical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are approved; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Change-capable access is explicitly approved — not assumed from being a sysadmin. Temporary elevated access for change windows is time-limited and approved.
commonEvidence: Access approval records for change-capable roles
Temporary access request records for change windows
Privileged account approval tickets

objectives.text: logical access restrictions associated with changes to the system are enforced.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement that non-authorized users cannot make configuration changes — RBAC, least-privilege configuration, PAM tools. Verified by confirming a non-admin account cannot install software or change system settings.
commonEvidence: Endpoint management platform confirming standard users lack install rights
PAM tool configuration restricting privileged actions
System audit logs showing rejected unauthorized change attempts

commonArtifacts: Physical access control policy and personnel list
Logical access matrix for change-capable roles
Group membership exports for change roles
Temporary access request and approval records
commonGaps: All IT staff can log into production systems with admin rights — no restriction on who can make changes
Physical access to server room is badge-controlled but the badge list is never reviewed or updated
Vendor and contractor temporary access is not time-limited or formally approved
Logical access restrictions defined in policy but not technically enforced
Change-capable accounts shared among team members — no individual accountability
Former employees' badge access disabled but logical admin access in patch management tools never revoked

title: Least Functionality
controlText: Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring organizational systems to provide only essential capabilities.
plainEnglish: Systems should be configured to do only what they need to do, nothing more. A web server does not need to run email services. A workstation does not need Bluetooth enabled if employees never use wireless peripherals. A database server does not need a web browser. Every unnecessary capability is a potential attack surface — removing it reduces risk without impacting the mission. This is a direct parallel to least privilege for accounts (AC.L2-3.1.5) applied to system capabilities instead.

objectives.text: essential system capabilities are defined based on the principle of least functionality; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented definition of what each system class is required to do — its business purpose and required capabilities. This list drives what is enabled; everything else is disabled by default. System role definitions per system class.
commonEvidence: System role definitions or capability matrix
Configuration Management Plan listing allowed capabilities per system type
Security configuration checklist with 'enabled/disabled' rationale per setting

objectives.text: the system is configured to provide only the defined essential capabilities.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical evidence that systems are actually configured to the defined capability set — not just a policy saying they should be. Compliance reports, configuration scans, or automated build outputs showing non-essential services are disabled.
commonEvidence: Endpoint hardening scan results showing disabled services
Network port scan results showing only expected ports open
Automated build pipeline outputs confirming baseline configuration
Services inventory showing only approved services running

commonArtifacts: System role definitions or capability matrix
Security configuration baselines with least-functionality implementation
commonGaps: Default operating system and application configurations used — many unnecessary services still running
Capability restrictions applied to servers but not to workstations or network devices
Bluetooth, LLMNR, NetBIOS, and other low-value protocols still enabled by default
Legacy applications require broad system access that creates unnecessary capability exposure
Least functionality defined in policy but no technical scan ever validates it
Cloud resources provisioned with default configurations, not trimmed to least functionality

title: Nonessential Functionality
controlText: Restrict, disable, or prevent the use of nonessential programs, functions, ports, protocols, and services.
plainEnglish: This extends CM.L2-3.4.6 (least functionality) by specifying exactly what must be restricted: programs, functions, ports, protocols, and services individually. CM.L2-3.4.6 establishes the principle; CM.L2-3.4.7 requires the specific technical implementation for each category. Unused ports blocked. Unnecessary protocols disabled (FTP, Telnet, LLMNR, NBT-NS). Peer-to-peer networking prevented. Non-essential services stopped and set to manual/disabled. Software restricted to the approved list.

objectives.text: essential programs are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented list of approved/essential programs per system type. This is the software whitelist or approved application catalog.
commonEvidence: Approved software list or application catalog
Application allow-list configuration
Software inventory from endpoint management tool

objectives.text: the use of nonessential programs is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented definition of what programs are NOT allowed — either an explicit deny list or a default-deny-all-not-on-the-approved-list approach.
commonEvidence: Prohibited software list or policy
Application control policy (blacklist vs whitelist approach)
Endpoint security software unauthorized application detection logs

objectives.text: the use of nonessential programs is restricted, disabled, or prevented as defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement that non-approved programs cannot run or are detected when they run. AppLocker, Defender Application Control, Carbon Black, or equivalent.
commonEvidence: Application control tool configuration and enforcement logs
AppLocker or WDAC policy export
Blocked application execution reports

objectives.text: essential functions are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of essential system functions that must remain enabled per the system's role.
commonEvidence: System role definition with required functions
Configuration baseline specifying enabled functions

objectives.text: the use of nonessential functions is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Explicit definition of which functions are not needed and will be disabled.
commonEvidence: Configuration checklist with disabled functions documented
Security baseline showing function-level disable decisions

objectives.text: the use of nonessential functions is restricted, disabled, or prevented as defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical verification that non-essential functions are actually disabled — not just documented as such. Configuration scan results.
commonEvidence: Configuration compliance scan results
GPO or MDM settings disabling specific OS functions

objectives.text: essential ports are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of required open ports per system type or segment, tied to specific business-justified services.
commonEvidence: Approved ports list or firewall rule documentation
Network architecture diagram with port mappings

objectives.text: the use of nonessential ports is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Explicit identification of ports to be blocked or closed. Not just 'everything else' — actually enumerated in the policy or configuration standard.
commonEvidence: Firewall default-deny policy with blocked port documentation
Host firewall configuration baseline

objectives.text: the use of nonessential ports is restricted, disabled, or prevented as defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Network scan showing only expected ports open. Host-based firewall scan showing non-essential ports blocked.
commonEvidence: Network port scan results (Nmap, Nessus, Qualys)
Host firewall compliance reports
Firewall rule audits

objectives.text: essential protocols are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of approved network protocols — those required for business operations.
commonEvidence: Approved protocols list in network security policy
Firewall rule documentation showing allowed protocols

objectives.text: the use of nonessential protocols is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Explicit prohibition of dangerous legacy protocols — FTP, Telnet, SSLv3, TLS 1.0/1.1, LLMNR, NetBIOS, NTLMv1, SNMPv1/v2.
commonEvidence: Protocol restriction policy
Network security configuration standard listing disabled protocols

objectives.text: the use of nonessential protocols is restricted, disabled, or prevented as defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical verification — TLS scan results, network scan, Group Policy settings disabling LLMNR/NetBIOS, SNMP version enforcement.
commonEvidence: TLS scan results showing disabled legacy versions
Group Policy settings disabling LLMNR, NetBIOS over TCP/IP
Network protocol analysis results

objectives.text: essential services are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of system services required for each system type to perform its role.
commonEvidence: Required services list per system class
Approved services baseline in configuration documentation

objectives.text: the use of nonessential services is defined; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Explicit list of services to be stopped and disabled — Windows services, Linux daemons, cloud platform services not needed for the workload's purpose.
commonEvidence: Disabled services list in hardening baseline
Configuration checklist with service enable/disable decisions

objectives.text: the use of nonessential services is restricted, disabled, or prevented as defined.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Running services list on representative systems shows only approved services. Automated scan confirms compliance.
commonEvidence: Running services audit output from representative systems
Configuration compliance scan showing service state
Endpoint management platform compliance report

commonArtifacts: Approved programs, ports, protocols, and services documentation
Group Policy and MDM settings for protocol and service restrictions
commonGaps: LLMNR and NetBIOS still enabled on workstations and servers — common lateral movement enabler
FTP or Telnet services still running on legacy systems with no documented exception
Application restriction policy is blacklist-based with no monitoring of unlisted software
Port restrictions apply to the perimeter but host-based firewalls are disabled or set to allow-all
Services audit done once at build time; no ongoing verification that services haven't re-enabled
SNMPv1/v2 still used for monitoring with community strings that amount to plaintext credentials

title: Application Execution Policy
controlText: Apply deny-by-exception (blacklisting) policy to prevent the use of unauthorized software or deny-all, permit-by-exception (whitelisting) policy to allow the execution of authorized software.
plainEnglish: This extends CM.L2-3.4.9 (which only requires controlling user-installed software) to require a system-level application execution policy. You must choose: either whitelist (nothing runs unless explicitly approved — stronger) or blacklist (everything can run unless explicitly blocked — weaker but more practical). The choice must be documented, and the configured list must match the documentation. This is a key ransomware and malware defense because it prevents execution of unauthorized payloads even if they somehow land on the system.

objectives.text: a policy specifying whether whitelisting or blacklisting is to be implemented is specified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented policy decision on application execution approach — whitelist or blacklist — with business rationale. The policy should specify any exceptions (e.g., whitelist on servers, blacklist on workstations).
commonEvidence: Application execution policy document
Configuration Management Policy section on application control
System security plan section on application execution

objectives.text: the software allowed to execute under whitelisting or denied use under blacklisting is specified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Maintained list of allowed applications (whitelist) or blocked applications (blacklist) — not just a policy saying there will be a list, but the actual list with version information where applicable.
commonEvidence: Application allow-list or deny-list export from control tool
AppLocker, WDAC, or Defender Application Control policy rules
EDR/AV blocked applications list

objectives.text: whitelisting to allow the execution of authorized software or blacklisting to prevent the use of unauthorized software is implemented as specified.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical implementation matching the documented policy. Whitelist mode: unapproved applications fail to launch and generate events. Blacklist mode: known-bad applications are blocked and alerted. Evidence of actual enforcement, not just configuration.
commonEvidence: Application control enforcement logs
Blocked execution events in SIEM or EDR
Audit mode reports showing scope before full enforcement
Test evidence showing unauthorized application is blocked

commonArtifacts: AppLocker, WDAC, or equivalent tool configuration
SIEM alerts for blocked execution attempts
commonGaps: Policy says whitelist but implementation is audit-mode only — nothing is actually blocked
Application control deployed on servers but not on workstations where most executions occur
Allow-list so broad (entire C:\Windows\, Program Files\) that it provides minimal protection
Blacklist approach with no process to add newly-discovered malicious software promptly
Application execution policy not integrated with software procurement — approved apps installed without updating the allow-list
No monitoring of blocked execution events — the control runs silently with no one reviewing alerts

title: User-Installed Software
plainEnglish: Standard users should not be able to install arbitrary software on organizational systems. When they can, they introduce unvetted applications, license risks, malware vectors, and configuration drift — and it usually happens faster than IT can detect it. This practice requires a policy defining what users may and may not install, technical controls that enforce it, and monitoring to detect when users install software anyway (which happens, especially on systems where users have more access than they should). It is lighter than the full application execution policy in CM.L2-3.4.8.

objectives.text: a policy for controlling the installation of software by users is established;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented policy defining what users may install — typically nothing without IT approval, with a defined approval process. May permit installs from an approved app store or self-service portal with pre-approved items.
commonEvidence: Acceptable use policy covering software installation
User-installed software policy
Software request and approval procedure

objectives.text: installation of software by users is controlled based on the established policy; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement that standard users lack installation privileges — Windows UAC enforced, standard user account (not local admin), software deployment only via managed channels (SCCM, Intune, Jamf). Spot-check: attempt to install software as a standard user and confirm it fails.
commonEvidence: Endpoint management configuration confirming standard users lack admin rights
MDM policy preventing sideloading on mobile devices
Software deployment tool showing IT-controlled deployment only
UAC configuration showing elevation required for installations

objectives.text: installation of software by users is monitored.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Detection capability for software installs — endpoint management platform reports on newly-installed software, SIEM alerts on software installation events, periodic software inventory reconciliation comparing current state to approved baseline.
commonEvidence: Endpoint management software inventory change reports
SIEM or EDR alerts on software installation events
Periodic software inventory reconciliation records
Change detection tool reports showing new software

commonArtifacts: Endpoint management platform configuration showing user privilege levels
SIEM or EDR software installation alerts
IT software request and approval workflow
commonGaps: Users have local admin rights on their own workstations — can install anything
Policy prohibits user installs but no technical enforcement exists
Software install monitoring generates alerts but nobody reviews them
Approved self-service portal exists but users have found workarounds
Mobile devices not covered — users sideload apps or use personal apps for work
Developer workstations given broad install rights with no monitoring, creating a policy exception that swallows the rule
